addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

3	This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
4	defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
5	describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.
6	
7	Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
8	permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
9	never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
10	anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
11	title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.
12	
13	Next Issue #: 2
14	
15	## Bugs
16	
17	### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART
18	
19	A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rdwr_vn100` invocation opening
20	the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
21	afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
22	by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
23	per reconnect — not a clean threshold — and the odds rise with
24	baud.
25	
26	The locus is narrow. The **in-session** baud switch (the `baud`
27	command sends `VNWRG,05` and talks at the new rate on the live
28	connection) was clean at every baud tested. Only the
29	**fresh-process reopen** at the new baud wedges.
30	
31	- First observation, 2026-06-26 (chained, *no* power cycle
32	  between switches):
33	    - `baud 57600` → verified; `bench --baud 57600` (fresh
34	      process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
35	    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` → verified (1 retry); `bench
36	      --baud 230400` (fresh process) → **clean, 40.0 Hz** once.
37	    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply;
38	      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both. Power
39	      cycle restored 115200 / 40 Hz.
40	- Controlled retest, 2026-06-26 (**fresh power cycle before each**
41	  baud, explicit `--baud`):
42	    - 57600 → switch verified (1 retry); fresh-process bench
43	      clean, 40.3 Hz.
44	    - 115200 (boots here) → fresh-process bench clean, 39.8 Hz.
45	    - 230400 → switch verified; fresh-process bench clean,
46	      40.0 Hz.
47	    - 921600 → switch verified; fresh-process bench → **wedged on
48	      the first reconnect** (no reply). Power cycle restored
49	      115200 / 40 Hz.
50	- All baud changes were volatile (no `--persist`), so every power
51	  cycle reverts to the flash default 115200 / 40 Hz.
52	- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects. 57600 and
53	  230400 each survived one clean reconnect from a fresh power
54	  cycle — but 230400 wedged in the chained run, so a clean device
55	  state helps and is not a guarantee. 921600 wedged immediately.
56	  None of the single clean runs is a reliability proof.
57	- The bench link is RS-232: a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C USB-C to
58	  RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps) into the
59	  VN-100's RS-232 port. 921600 is ~2× that rating. We think the
60	  cause is RS-232 slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's
61	  transceiver and on the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver,
62	  neither swappable — mis-framing bytes into the device at high
63	  baud, which its firmware reacts to by wedging. It is *not* an
64	  FTDI control-line transient: no handshake lines are wired. Full
65	  analysis: chores-01 [[1]].
66	- This is an RS-232-rig finding. The RPi5 flight target uses a
67	  direct 3.3 V TTL UART with no RS-232 transceivers in the path,
68	  so the wedge must be retested there before it is treated as a
69	  VN-100 limit. We think it may not reproduce on TTL.
70	
71	**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
72	that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
73	ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
74	power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
75	200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
76	higher device baud.
77	
78	# References
79	
80	[1]: chores/chores-01.md#rs-232-link-analysis
81

# Bugs

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It lists known
defects we're aware of but haven't scheduled a fix for. Each entry
describes what goes wrong, when, and the cost of the failure.

Each bug is a `### Issue #N — <title>` subsection. The Issue # is a
permanent, monotonic id — assigned once from the allocator below,
never reused or renumbered — so the heading doubles as a stable
anchor (`#issue-N-…`) that todo / chores / commits can link to. The
title may be reworded; the `issue-N` prefix of the anchor stays put.

Next Issue #: 2

## Bugs

### Issue #1 — high-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART

A cross-process reconnect (a fresh `rdwr_vn100` invocation opening
the port) at a high baud can wedge the VN-100's UART completely:
afterward the device is silent at *every* baud, recoverable only
by a power cycle. The failure is intermittent and probabilistic
per reconnect — not a clean threshold — and the odds rise with
baud.

The locus is narrow. The **in-session** baud switch (the `baud`
command sends `VNWRG,05` and talks at the new rate on the live
connection) was clean at every baud tested. Only the
**fresh-process reopen** at the new baud wedges.

- First observation, 2026-06-26 (chained, *no* power cycle
  between switches):
    - `baud 57600` → verified; `bench --baud 57600` (fresh
      process) → clean, 40.0 Hz.
    - `--baud 57600 baud 230400` → verified (1 retry); `bench
      --baud 230400` (fresh process) → **clean, 40.0 Hz** once.
    - The very next fresh-process open at 230400 → no reply;
      `--baud 115200 get-hz` → no reply. Silent at both. Power
      cycle restored 115200 / 40 Hz.
- Controlled retest, 2026-06-26 (**fresh power cycle before each**
  baud, explicit `--baud`):
    - 57600 → switch verified (1 retry); fresh-process bench
      clean, 40.3 Hz.
    - 115200 (boots here) → fresh-process bench clean, 39.8 Hz.
    - 230400 → switch verified; fresh-process bench clean,
      40.0 Hz.
    - 921600 → switch verified; fresh-process bench → **wedged on
      the first reconnect** (no reply). Power cycle restored
      115200 / 40 Hz.
- All baud changes were volatile (no `--persist`), so every power
  cycle reverts to the flash default 115200 / 40 Hz.
- 115200 has a clean record across many reconnects. 57600 and
  230400 each survived one clean reconnect from a fresh power
  cycle — but 230400 wedged in the chained run, so a clean device
  state helps and is not a guarantee. 921600 wedged immediately.
  None of the single clean runs is a reliability proof.
- The bench link is RS-232: a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C USB-C to
  RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps) into the
  VN-100's RS-232 port. 921600 is ~2× that rating. We think the
  cause is RS-232 slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's
  transceiver and on the VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver,
  neither swappable — mis-framing bytes into the device at high
  baud, which its firmware reacts to by wedging. It is *not* an
  FTDI control-line transient: no handshake lines are wired. Full
  analysis: chores-01 [[1]].
- This is an RS-232-rig finding. The RPi5 flight target uses a
  direct 3.3 V TTL UART with no RS-232 transceivers in the path,
  so the wedge must be retested there before it is treated as a
  VN-100 limit. We think it may not reproduce on TTL.

**Cost / why it matters:** an intermittent *unrecoverable* failure
that passes bench testing is the worst kind for flight use — it
ships, then wedges the IMU mid-flight with no recovery but cutting
power. "Works sometimes" is not safe. Binary output already gives
200 Hz at the rock-solid 115200, so there is no reason to chase a
higher device baud.

# References

[1]: chores/chores-01.md#rs-232-link-analysis

<system-reminder>This memory is 5 days old. Memories are point-in-time observations, not live state — claims about code behavior or file:line citations may be outdated. Verify against current code before asserting as fact.</system-reminder>
1	---
2	name: rdwr-vn100-project
3	description: State and key findings for the rdwr_vn100 VN-100 IMU tool and the fc.py 200 Hz goal
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: aa4bfd5d-c6b3-4f32-ac00-4dca6460bfe2
8	---
9	
10	`rdwr_vn100` is a Rust CLI (in /home/wink/data/prgs/nps-gnc/rdwr_vn100) the user built to read/configure a VectorNav **VN-100** IMU over serial (`/dev/ttyUSB0`, an **FTDI FT232R**). Subcommands: `get`, `set <hz> [--persist]`, `baud <n> [--persist]`, `reset`, `factory-reset`, `bench [--hz][--secs]`, `help`. VN ASCII protocol `$VN...*XX` (XOR checksum); binary output uses `vn_crc16` (CCITT). 22 unit tests pass.
11	
12	**Real goal:** get the user's flight controller `../fc/src/fc.py` (symlink → `fc-current.py`) to read the IMU at **200 Hz** instead of the 40 Hz it currently gets.
13	
14	**Root cause of the 40 Hz (confirmed):** `VecNavHandler` uses the vendor SDK's `Sensor.autoConnect()` (probes [115200, 921600, …], finds device at its default 115200) and **never writes the device output rate** — `rate=200`/`baudrate=921600` params are effectively no-ops. So the device streams its default 40 Hz VNYMR. 40 Hz = VN-100 factory default async rate.
15	
16	**Why 200 Hz failed at 115200:** it's a *bandwidth* limit, not a frequency one. Default VNYMR ASCII ≈115 B; 200 Hz × 115 B ≈ 230 kbit/s > the ~115 kbit/s link → device returns `$VNERR,0C` (InsufficientBaudRate). Same reason `set 100` failed but 40/50 worked.
17

Reacquainted. Here's where things stand.

## Project: `rdwr_vn100`
Rust CLI to read/configure a VectorNav **VN-100** IMU over serial. The real goal behind it: get the flight controller reading the IMU at **200 Hz** instead of the 40 Hz it gets today.

**The core result is already proven:** stay at rock-solid **115200 baud** and switch the device to a **compact binary output** (reg 75, Common group, rateDivisor=4 → 200 Hz, ~26 B/frame). `bench` measured exactly 200.0 Hz, all CRC-valid. No need to chase higher device baud.

## Repo state
- Clean working copy. `@` is an empty commit on top of `main` (`656853e`).
- **Todo / In Progress lists are empty** — just scaffolding template text.
- Last commit `656853e` *docs: notes scaffolding + RS-232 wedge findings* landed AGENTS.md + the full notes/ structure + the first investigation.
- Note: since the memory was written, the CLI was generalized (`fa3b82f`) — `get`/`set` were renamed to **`get-hz`/`set-hz`**, and `rrg`/`wrg` + bench modes added. So the memory's command names are stale.

## Open item — Bug Issue #1 (the live thread)
**High-baud reconnect can wedge the VN-100 UART** — a fresh-process reopen at high baud can leave the device silent at *every* baud, recoverable only by power cycle. Intermittent/probabilistic, odds rise with baud:
- 115200 = clean across many reconnects (and gives 200 Hz via binary anyway).
- 230400 survived once, wedged once. 921600 wedged immediately.
- Root cause attributed to the **RS-232 bench link** (Gearmo USB↔RS-232 adapter + VN-100's onboard RS-232 transceiver slew-rate limiting at ~2× rated baud), *not* an FTDI control-line transient.
- **Caveat carried forward:** this is an RS-232-rig finding. The **RPi5 flight target uses direct 3.3 V TTL UART** (no RS-232 transceivers), so the wedge must be retested there before treating it as a real VN-100 limit. We think it may not reproduce on TTL.

## Possible next directions (none scheduled)
- The `fcbr` idea — "flight controller, binary, Rust" — reimplement the FC read path in Rust on the proven binary output, instead of patching `fc.py`.
- Independent verification of the 200 Hz (vendor SDK + raw pyserial).
- RPi5/TTL retest of the wedge.

content.text:
Read the bench code (`bench_binary` at `src/main.rs:1035`, `bench_ascii` at `:1115`). I like the direction. Here's my read.

## What makes bench complex today
Both paths do the same three-act dance: **read prev state → write new config → measure → write prev back**. That's where the weight is:
- `bench_binary` remembers reg 07, writes reg 75 (binary on), writes reg 07=0 (silence ASCII), measures, then writes reg 75 off + reg 07 back.
- `bench_ascii` remembers reg 07 (and optionally reg 06), writes new, measures, restores.
- Every restore is a `let _ = transact_retry(...)` — best-effort, swallowed errors. So restore can silently half-fail and you wouldn't know.

And separately, every one of those writes is *exactly* the device-mutating operation that Issue #1 ties to the wedge risk. The common question — "what's the device streaming right now?" — currently can't be answered without mutating the device.

## Your proposal, restated
- **No args → passive measure.** Open the port, count whatever's already flowing for N seconds, report the rate. Zero writes, no restore needed, no wedge surface.
- **A setting passed → configure it, measure, restore to the prior ("current") value.** Restore becomes *conditional* — it only runs when you actually changed something, which is the only time it's needed.

I think that's the right split. It collapses the no-arg common case to a pure read and confines the read→write→restore machinery to the case that asked for it.

## One wrinkle to decide on
Passive measurement needs to know *what* it's counting:
- **ASCII is trivial** — count `$VN…` lines (`is_async_line` already exists at `:943`).
- **Binary is not** — to count/decode frames passively you need the frame length, which means reading **reg 75** and reconstructing the field list (new code: parse reg 75 → fields → `frame_len`). Today `frame_len` is only known because *you* passed `--fields`.

So two ways to scope the passive path:
1. **Passive = byte throughput + ASCII message rate only.** Simplest. If the device is in binary mode, you report bytes/s but not a decoded frame rate. Binary decode stays exclusively in the explicit `--bin` (active) path.
2. **Passive introspects reg 75** to reconstruct binary framing and report true frame rate. More faithful, but adds the reg-75 parser — some of the complexity you're cutting comes back, just on the read side.

I'd lean toward **(1)** to start: it's the genuine simplification and matches "measure whatever's current" honestly. Add reg-75 introspection later only if you find yourself wanting decoded binary rate without reconfiguring.

## Net
Agree it's too complex, and your framing targets the right thing — the restore/reconfigure dance, not the measuring. The main design call is just how smart the passive binary case needs to be (wrinkle above). Want me to sketch the parse_args / command-shape change for either option, still thoughts-only?

`bench` currently takes these five parameters (plus the global `--port`/`--baud`):

| Param | Default | Applies to | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `--bin` | off (ASCII) | — | Selects binary output (reg 75) instead of ASCII async. |
| `--hz HZ` | `40` | both | Rate to **configure** before measuring. ASCII: must be in `VALID_RATES`. Binary: must divide 800 (50/100/200/400…). |
| `--secs S` | `5` | both | Measurement duration. |
| `--fields LIST` | `time,accel` | binary only | Comma-separated fields for the binary frame; rejected without `--bin`. |
| `--type NAME` | none | ASCII only | Sets the ASCII message preset (reg 6) first; rejected with `--bin`. |

So every parameter except `--secs` is about **configuring** the device, not measuring it. `--bin`, `--hz`, `--fields`, `--type` all feed the write-then-restore dance. Only `--secs` is a pure measurement knob.

That lines up exactly with your proposal: under your split, `--secs` stays on the passive path (it just controls how long you watch), and `--bin` / `--hz` / `--fields` / `--type` become the "you passed a setting → configure it, then restore" group. The no-arg case (just `--secs`, optional) drops all four config params and all the restore code.

Worth noting: today `bench` with no flags **isn't** passive — it defaults to `--hz 40` ASCII and *writes* reg 07=40 before measuring. So even the bare `bench` mutates the device right now. Your change would flip that default from "configure to 40 Hz ASCII" to "measure what's already there."

content.text:
I like this a lot better than my conditional-restore idea — it's a cleaner decomposition. A few things to weigh, though.

## The good
- **bench becomes a pure read** — `bench [secs]`, no device writes, no wedge surface, no restore. That's the safe common case made trivial.
- **The restore dance disappears entirely.** And it disappears *consistently*: `set-hz` already doesn't restore, so `set-bin`/`set-ascii` not restoring matches the rest of the CLI. get/set is a model the tool already uses (`get-hz`/`set-hz`, `rrg`/`wrg`).
- Each command maps to one register concern, so the field-mask / preset / divisor machinery each lives in exactly one place instead of being tangled into bench.

## The one behavior you're dropping — call it out
Today `bench` cleans up after itself (measures, then puts the device back). Under this design, "quick binary bench, leave the device as I found it" becomes a manual 3-step: `get-bin` (note state) → `set-bin …` → `bench` → set it back. I think that's an acceptable trade — explicit and predictable beats magic restore, and the device is volatile anyway (power cycle reverts non-persisted changes). But it *is* a real change in habit, worth naming.

## Carve-up questions to settle
1. **How do `get-ascii`/`set-ascii` relate to the existing `get-hz`/`set-hz`?** The ASCII async output is governed by *two* registers:
   - reg **6** = message *type/preset* (ADOR: YPR, QTN, YMR…)
   - reg **7** = async *rate* (ADOF) — already owned by `set-hz`.

   So `set-ascii` should own reg 6 (the preset), and `set-hz` keeps reg 7. Clean, but it means "configure the ASCII output" is split across two verbs. Acceptable if `get-ascii` *reports both* (type + rate) so there's one place to see the full picture.

2. **`set-bin` needs a rate, not just fields.** Reg 75 is fields **+** rateDivisor (+ which serial port). So it's really `set-bin <FIELDS> [--hz HZ]` (default 200?), and `get-bin` should report fields, rate, port, and frame length.

3. **The `<field/mode>` asymmetry is real and worth making explicit in the names/help.** Binary takes *composable fields* (`set-bin time,accel,gyro` → bitmask). ASCII takes a *single preset mode* (`set-ascii ymr`), not arbitrary fields. So it's `set-bin <FIELDS>` vs `set-ascii <MODE>` — don't let the symmetry of the names imply they take the same kind of argument.

4. **`off`** — bench_binary currently silences ASCII (reg 7 = 0) so it measures only binary. In the new world that's just `set-ascii off` (reg 6 = 0) by hand. Probably want an `off` mode on both `set-ascii` and `set-bin`.

## The passive-binary wrinkle — now mostly solved
Earlier I flagged that passive measurement can't report a binary *frame rate* without knowing the frame length. With `get-bin` existing, that's no longer a blocker: bench can reuse the same reg-75 parser to self-configure its frame counter. Or sniff the stream — VN binary frames start with sync byte `0xFA`, ASCII with `$` — and pick a counter automatically. Simplest v1: bench always reports byte throughput, plus ASCII message rate via `$VN` line count, and decoded binary rate when it can read reg 75. Your call on how smart v1 needs to be.

## Sketch of the resulting surface
```
bench [SECS]              passive measure of whatever's streaming (default 5s)
get-hz / set-hz <HZ>      reg 7  — ASCII async rate            (exists)
get-ascii / set-ascii <MODE|off>   reg 6  — ASCII message preset
get-bin   / set-bin <FIELDS|off> [--hz HZ]   reg 75 — binary output
```

Net: I'd go with it. The four open calls are (1) reg6-vs-reg7 split between `set-ascii` and `set-hz`, (2) `set-bin` carrying a rate, (3) keeping the fields-vs-mode argument asymmetry visible, and (4) how smart passive-binary measurement needs to be in v1. Want me to draft the `parse_args` / `Command` enum shape for this, or keep it at thoughts?

content.text:
This is where the design gets genuinely interesting, because one of these three pulls the other two together — and it happens to attack Issue #1 directly.

## The keystone: multiple commands per invocation
The reason this matters beyond convenience: **each command today is a separate process = a separate port open = a separate wedge die-roll** (Issue #1 fires on fresh-process reopens at high baud). A sequence run in *one* process opens the port **once**:

```
rdwr_vn100 save-state set-bin=time,accel bench restore-state   # ONE open
```

vs. four invocations = four reconnects. So multi-command isn't just ergonomics — it's the safest way to do a configure→measure→restore cycle, and it's what lets `bench` stay purely passive while still supporting the "measure binary then put it back" workflow we removed from bench. That's the strongest argument in this whole thread. I'd treat it as the keystone and build the other two on top.

## The `=` syntax — yes, and here's its actual job
`set-hz=200` isn't just style; it makes each command **self-delimiting**, which is exactly what a sequence parser needs. Without it, `set-hz 200 set-ascii ymr` forces the parser to know each command's arity to find token boundaries. With `=`, every token carries its own argument. So the `=` syntax is *motivated by* the multi-command feature — adopt them together, and use `=` for the globals too (`--baud=921600`) so there's one rule.

**One wrinkle:** `set-bin` is multi-valued (fields **and** a rate). `set-hz=200` fits `cmd=value` cleanly; `set-bin` doesn't. You'll need a sub-syntax decision — e.g. `set-bin=time,accel@200` or `set-bin=200:time,accel`. Don't let it fall back to a separate `--hz` token or the sequence ambiguity returns.

## save-state / restore-state — decide the scope first
There are two very different versions:

- **(a) In-memory, within one invocation.** `save-state` reads the relevant regs (5 baud / 6 ascii-type / 7 rate / 75 binary) into the running process; `restore-state` writes them back before exit. No filesystem. This is *exactly* bench's old auto-restore — but now explicit, general, and composable with any set-combo. Pairs perfectly with the keystone.
- **(b) File-backed, across invocations.** `save-state` persists a snapshot to disk; `restore-state` reads it back tomorrow. More powerful, but adds config-file concerns (where it lives, format, staleness if the device changed underneath it).

I'd do **(a) first**. It's the clean replacement for what we're removing and needs no new persistence story. Note the caveat it implies: standalone `rdwr_vn100 save-state` (alone) is a no-op — it only means something paired with `restore-state` in the same sequence. If you want standalone save to be meaningful, that's the argument for (b) — but I'd defer it until there's a real need.

The convenience save-state buys over typing it yourself: you don't have to *know* the current values — it reads "whatever it is now" and puts it back. Same value bench's restore had.

## New questions the sequence model forces
1. **Error semantics mid-sequence.** If `set-bin` fails on step 2 of 4, abort the rest? I'd say **fail-fast and report which step** — but then a failed sequence may leave the device half-configured and `restore-state` never runs. Worth thinking about whether restore-state should run on a best-effort "always, even on earlier failure" basis (like a `defer`).
2. **Non-composable commands.** `reset` / `factory-reset` reboot the device — anything after them hits a rebooting device. Constrain them to be standalone (or last).
3. **Ordering is now the user's job.** `save-state` first, `restore-state` last. Fine, just explicit — but the help should model the canonical order.

## Gut-check on scope
This turns the CLI from one-command-per-process into a **small batch executor** (sequence + state snapshot + token syntax). That's real scope growth from "bench is too complex" — but I think it's *justified* growth, because the one-open-per-sequence property is the cleanest answer to the wedge risk you've been worried about all along. So it's not feature creep; it's the feature that makes passive-bench viable without losing restore.

Net: I'd commit to all three, in this order of dependency — multi-command sequence (keystone) → `=` syntax (its disambiguator) → in-memory save/restore-state (the composable restore). Defer file-backed state and decide the `set-bin` sub-syntax + the fail-fast/defer-restore semantics before coding.

Want me to draft the `Command` sequence + `parse_args` shape (returning `Vec<Command>` with the `=` tokenizer), still thoughts-only?

content.text:
KIS makes `set-bin` single-valued, which kills the sub-syntax wrinkle I was worried about — good. But it surfaces a real asymmetry between the two modes that's worth pinning down before it bites.

## The two things you clarified — both good
- **`set-bin=time,accel` = fields only**, rate comes from a separate `set-hz`. So no `@200`/`:200` sub-syntax — clean.
- **Bare `set-bin` = "enable binary with whatever fields are already set."** Nice default, and it's symmetric: bare `set-ascii` = "enable ASCII with the current preset." KIS. (You'll still want an explicit `set-bin=off` to disable, since the bare form now means "enable as-is" rather than "toggle.")

## The catch: ASCII rate and binary rate don't live in the same place
This is the one thing "rate is separate" runs into. The registers aren't symmetric:

- **ASCII**: type is reg **6**, rate is reg **7** — genuinely two separate registers. So `set-ascii=ymr set-hz=200` maps 1:1 to two independent register writes. "Separate" is literally true here.
- **Binary**: the rate is the **rateDivisor inside reg 75**, *the same register as the fields* (`VNWRG,75,1,<divisor>,01,<mask>`). There is no separate "binary rate register." So `set-bin=time,accel set-hz=200` can't be two independent writes — the tool has to gather the fields from `set-bin` **and** the rate from `set-hz`, compute `divisor = 800/hz`, and emit **one** combined reg-75 write.

That's resolvable and can still look clean to you, but it changes the execution model: it's not pure "each command = one register poke, left to right." For binary you need a **resolve-then-apply** step — collect all the set-* intents, then emit the reg-75 write once with both pieces. Two ways to frame it:

- **(A) `set-hz` is the universal "desired Hz"** — ASCII → reg 7, binary → folded into the reg-75 divisor. Prettiest for you (`set-hz=200` always means 200 Hz), at the cost of that internal merge for binary. My lean.
- **(B) `set-hz` is ASCII-only (reg 7)**; binary rate is its own thing (back inside `set-bin`, or a `set-bin-hz`). Keeps one-command-one-register literally true, but then binary rate *isn't* `set-hz`, which fights the "rate is separate, uniform" idea.

I'd go **(A)** — it's the KIS *user* model even though it costs a small resolve step internally. Just know bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` then means "keep the current divisor too," and `set-bin=fields` + `set-hz=200` recomputes it.

## One ordering question
Your example is `bench set-bin=time,accel set-hz=200` — **bench first**. If the sequence runs left-to-right (the KIS/predictable model), bench would measure the *old* config and then reconfigure — probably not what you want. The natural form would be sets first, measure last:

```
rdwr_vn100 set-hz=200 set-bin=time,accel bench
```

So: do you want **strict left-to-right** (then bench belongs last), or is **`bench` special-cased to always run after configuration** (order-independent, but more magic)? I'd vote left-to-right — predictable, and it composes with `save-state … restore-state` wrapping the whole line.

Net: `set-bin`=fields-only and bare-`set-bin`=current-fields are both clean wins. The only thing to decide is (A) vs (B) for where binary rate lives, and whether execution is strictly left-to-right. Want me to draft the resolve-then-apply execution shape for option (A), thoughts-only?

content.text:
Yes, that makes sense and resolves the ordering cleanly. Let me play it back to confirm we're aligned:

## Settled model
- **Option A**: `set-hz=N` is a *universal desired rate*. ASCII → reg 7. Binary → folded into reg 75's divisor (`800/N`).
- **Execution is a fixed pipeline, not literal left-to-right command execution.** The CLI line is parsed into a desired-state accumulator; the *verbs* then run in a fixed phase order regardless of where they sit on the line:

  1. `save-state` (if present) — snapshot current regs to memory
  2. **apply** the accumulated `set-*` config (one resolved reg-75 write for binary, reg 6/7 for ASCII)
  3. `bench` (if present) — measure once, passively
  4. `restore-state` (if present) — write the snapshot back

- **`set-*` tokens accumulate left-to-right; last write wins on a duplicate key** (`set-hz=100 set-hz=200` → 200). So position only matters for resolving conflicts, not for sequencing the action.
- `bench` is optional (config-only lines are valid) and runs at most once; its `secs` rides on the token (`bench=10`, default 5).

So `bench set-bin=time,accel set-hz=200` and `set-hz=200 set-bin=time,accel bench` do the identical thing — bench always measures the post-config state.

## Two things still genuinely open
1. **`set-bin` and `set-ascii` both on one line** — the VN-100 *can* stream both at once, but for a bench that's ambiguous about what you're measuring. Error out, or accept both and measure total throughput? I'd lean error ("pick one output to bench") unless you have a reason to want both.
2. **Persist** — apply as volatile (matches today's bench, reverts on power cycle), with `--persist` available, or always volatile? Probably volatile-by-default; flag for later.

Neither blocks the shape.

## How do you want to proceed?
This is a real CLI redesign, so per the cycle protocol it wants a Todo/cycle entry before code. Three options — your call, I'll not touch anything until you say:
- **Capture it** — I draft a `notes/todo.md` entry (and a chores design subsection for the pipeline/option-A rationale) so it's recorded, then we implement later.
- **Implement now** — I open a cycle and start the `parse_args` → accumulator → pipeline rework.
- **Keep refining** — settle the two open items first.

content.text:
Got it — that's a meaningful correction. Let me re-slot them.

So there are really **two token categories**, and save-state/restore-state are in the second, same as bench:

- **Config parameters** — `set-hz=`, `set-bin=`, `set-ascii=` — accumulate into the desired-state, then apply as one config step.
- **Action commands** — `bench`, `save-state`, `restore-state`, `get-bin`, `get-ascii`, `reset`, … — things the tool *does*.

That makes the bench pipeline simpler than what I drew: it's just **apply `set-*` → `bench` (once)**. No save/restore wrapping baked into bench.

## The consequence to nail down
If `save-state` and `restore-state` are standalone commands — runnable in their *own* invocation:

```
rdwr_vn100 save-state
rdwr_vn100 set-bin=time,accel set-hz=200 bench
rdwr_vn100 restore-state
```

— then the snapshot has to **persist to a file**, because each invocation is a fresh process with nothing held in memory. That's the file-backed version (option B from earlier), and it flips my prior lean toward in-memory. Which is fine — file-backed is the right call *if* they stand alone, and it works uniformly whether you run them standalone or chain them on one line (`save-state … restore-state` just round-trips through the file). KIS: always file-backed, one behavior.

## So, three things to confirm
1. **File-backed is intended** — `save-state` writes a snapshot file (regs 5/6/7/75), `restore-state` reads it back and writes to the device. Yes?
2. **Where / format** — a fixed path like `~/.config/rdwr_vn100/state.json` (or `~/.local/state/…`)? And single global file, or keyed by port (matters only if you ever run two VN-100s)?
3. **Do the action commands still chain on one line**, or is each its own invocation? Both work with file-backed state — but it decides whether we still build the multi-command sequencer at all, or each invocation runs exactly one action command (+ its accumulated `set-*`).

That last one is the real fork: if you're happy running save-state / bench / restore-state as three separate invocations, the parser stays simple (one action command per line + `set-*` params), and we drop the whole multi-command-sequence machinery. Simpler — at the cost of three port opens instead of one. At 115200 that's clean per Issue #1, so it may be the right trade. Where's your head on chaining vs. one-action-per-invocation?

message.content: 1. yes files, 2. default=./.rdwr_vn100-config.toml override via cli, 3. I hope they chain and it would be one connection.

content.text:
All three settled, good. Quick confirmations + one last sequencing question that chaining forces.

## Confirmed
1. **File-backed** snapshot.
2. **Default `./.rdwr_vn100-config.toml`**, override via a CLI flag — call it `--config=PATH`. Bonus: TOML means the file is hand-editable, so `restore-state` doubles as "apply a config I wrote by hand," which fits the filename nicely.
3. **Chaining over one connection** — so we *do* build the multi-command sequencer, one port open per line. That's the wedge-safe path (Issue #1), and it's the payoff that justifies the sequencer's existence.

## The one thing chaining still leaves open
Action commands run **left-to-right** in one connection — `save-state set-bin=time,accel set-hz=200 bench restore-state`. But `set-*` are *collected* (we agreed: gather all of them, last-wins, then resolve the one reg-75 write for option A). So: **when does that single resolved config-write actually hit the device, relative to the actions around it?** It has to land *after* `save-state` (so the snapshot is the old state) and *before* `bench`/`restore-state`.

Cleanest rule I can offer: **the run of `set-*` tokens collapses to one "apply config" step that executes at its position in the line.** So in `save-state set-bin=… set-hz=… bench restore-state`:
- `save-state` → snapshot old regs
- `set-bin`+`set-hz` → resolved into **one** reg-75 write, applied here
- `bench` → measure the new config
- `restore-state` → write the snapshot back

That keeps it left-to-right and intuitive (config applies where you wrote the settings), while still letting set-bin + set-hz merge into a single binary write. Does that match your mental model, or did you picture config applying at a fixed point (e.g. always right before bench) regardless of token position?

## Two small flags, not blockers
- **What's in the snapshot.** Regs 6 (ASCII type), 7 (rate), 75 (binary) are clearly in. **Baud (reg 5) is the dangerous one** — `restore-state` rewriting baud means a baud change, which is exactly the wedge trigger. I'd lean: snapshot/restore the output config (6/7/75) but **leave baud out** of restore by default, since `--baud` already controls the link. Your call.
- **`reset`/`factory-reset` in a chain** reboot the device mid-sequence — anything after them talks to a rebooting device. Constrain them to standalone/last.

Where's your head on the config-apply position? Once that's pinned, I think the design's complete enough to either capture in `notes/todo.md` or start a cycle.

content.text:
Ooh, I like this more than my implicit "config applies somewhere" hand-wave — because making the grouping *explicit* kills the magic. Let me reflect back what I think you mean, then the thing it unlocks.

## The model I'm reading
- Whole line = **one invocation = one open connection** (wedge-safe, the whole point).
- The line is a sequence of **steps** executed in order. **Space = step boundary** ("execute what I've gathered, move on").
- Within a step, tokens **joined by `+`/`:` apply together as one atomic unit.**

Falls out of shell word-splitting for free: each shell *word* is a step; the joiner lives inside a word (no spaces), so `set-hz=100+set-baud=921600+save-state` arrives as one argv element = one step, and `bench` is the next.

## The payoff — this isn't just cosmetic
The flat "collect all set-\* globally, run one bench" model can only do **one** config+measure per line. Explicit steps let you do **many in one connection** — a rate sweep, which is exactly this project's bread and butter (200 Hz benchmarking) and does it wedge-safely on a single open:

```
rdwr_vn100 save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench restore-state
```

Five steps, one connection: snapshot → (set 50, measure) → (set 100, measure) → (set 200, measure) → restore. The flat model can't express that. That alone sells me on the step idea.

## It also resolves the option-A coupling cleanly
No more "where does the accumulated config apply?" guesswork. The join *is* the answer: `set-bin=time,accel+set-hz=200` means "these two merge into the single reg-75 write." Same word = same device write. Different words = different steps. The grouping you type is the batching the device gets.

## `:` vs `+` — I'd pick `+`
- `+` reads as "and also," and is low-collision. `:` shows up in `PATH`-style values, time-ish strings, Windows drive letters, and could clash with a `--config=` path. Both are shell-safe unquoted, but `+` has less chance of biting a value.
- So: `set-hz=200+bench`, `set-bin=time,accel+set-hz=200`.

## Two wrinkles to keep honest
1. **Intra-step order is left-to-right too.** `set-hz=50+bench` = apply, *then* measure. And `save-state` must land *before* any set step (snapshot the old state) — so it's its own leading step, not joined after the sets. Your earlier `…+save-state` at the *end* of a set group would snapshot the already-changed state, which is backwards.
2. **KIS counterpoint, said plainly.** This is a custom two-tier syntax (steps + joiner). It earns its keep *if* you want sweeps / explicit grouping. If in practice every line is just "one config, one bench," the flat left-to-right model is less to learn. So the deciding question is really: **do you want multi-step sweeps in a single connection?** If yes — and for a 200 Hz bench tool I think you do — the step model is worth the syntax.

So my lean: adopt the step model with `+`, because the one-connection sweep is genuinely useful here and the explicit join removes the apply-position ambiguity entirely. Want to keep free-thinking, or is this the point to write the whole thing up as a design note before it evaporates?

Also we should be able to name states set-state=fast,set-hz=200,set-baud=921600 set-state=slow,set-hz=1,set-baud=115200,set-state this last one sets a named state and then makes it the default, more free thinking. content.text:
Two parts here, and they have very different "falls out for free" answers — let me be honest about which is which.

## Part 1: ordering flexibility — yes, free
`restore-state` first to re-run the same test, `save-state` first in the typical case — that just falls out of left-to-right step execution. No special-casing, agreed. Zero cost. ✓

## Part 2: named states — the *data* is free, the *grammar* isn't
Pull these apart:

**The storage model falls out beautifully.** The TOML file you already specified just becomes a map of named profiles:
```toml
default = "slow"
[states.fast] hz = 200, baud = 921600
[states.slow] hz = 1,  baud = 115200
```
For a bench tool, *named profiles are a killer feature* — `fast`, `slow`, `flight`, `wedge-repro` — and they cost almost nothing because the file's already there. I'm sold on this part.

**The command surface does NOT fall out for free, though.** Three real decisions hiding in your example:

1. **Comma collides.** You wrote `set-state=fast,set-hz=200,set-baud=921600` — but comma is already taken by `set-bin=time,accel` (field lists). A flat parser can't tell "next token" from "next field." We just picked `+` as the joiner two turns ago; keep it, and reserve comma for value-internal lists:
   ```
   set-state=fast+set-hz=200+set-baud=921600
   ```
   The nested alternative (`set-state=fast,set-hz=200,…` where the value itself contains `key=val` pairs) is the *harder* parse — it makes a value contain sub-assignments, i.e. recursion. `+` keeps everything flat and top-level. So: not free, but cheap if we stay with `+`.

2. **Three verbs, three distinct jobs — keep them from blurring.** You've now got:
   - `save-state=NAME` — capture *from the device* into NAME.
   - `set-state=NAME+…` — define NAME *from explicit values* (no device read).
   - `restore-state=NAME` — apply NAME *to the device*.

   All three are genuinely useful and genuinely different (capture vs declare vs apply). But that's a verb matrix that needs crisp, non-overlapping definitions or it turns into soup. That's design work, not zero.

3. **"Make it the default" — careful, this one's a safety trap.** If "default" means *"the profile `restore-state` uses when you give no name"* → great, safe, cheap. If it means *"auto-applied on every invocation"* → that makes **every run write to the device**, which is exactly the surprise-mutation / wedge risk (Issue #1) we've spent this whole thread keeping the tool *away* from. The tool's safety story is "passive by default, writes only when asked." A default that auto-applies breaks that. So: define "default" as the no-name target for `restore-state`, **not** an auto-apply. Then bare `restore-state` = apply the default profile.

## Net on "zero complication"
- **Profile storage:** essentially free, and a great feature. ✅
- **Grammar + verbs + default semantics:** *modest but real* — a joiner choice (keep `+`), three crisp verb definitions, and one safety guardrail. Not free, but not heavy either.

So: yes do it, but it's "small deliberate design," not "falls out of the parser." 

One meta-thought: we've now designed a *lot* across these turns — passive bench, `set-*`/`get-*` decomposition, file-backed state, the `+`-step grammar, sweeps, named profiles, defaults. It's all still only in this conversation. Before it evaporates, want me to write it up as a design note (a `notes/todo.md` entry + a chores design subsection capturing the grammar and the rationale)? You can keep free-thinking on top of a written spec instead of holding it all in your head.

  C++ SDK — `rdwr_vn100` already replaces it by talking the

  How it then feeds `fc.py` (IPC or a PyO3 module) is a later
  boundary question; a full Rust rewrite (the `fcbr` direction)
  is wanted eventually, not now.

### RS-232 link analysis

The bench adapter is a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C — a USB-C to true
RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps on the spec

## feat: passive bench, composable command grammar

Commits:

Today's `bench` is too complex: every run *mutates* the device
(configure → measure → restore), conflating measurement with
configuration, and each subcommand is its own process — one port
open per invocation, and each fresh-process reopen is a wedge
die-roll [[1]]. The redesign makes `bench` purely passive, breaks
configuration into composable `get-*`/`set-*` verbs, runs a whole
CLI line over a single connection, and adds file-backed named
states. We think running the line over one open cuts the
reconnect count that Issue #1 ties to wedges.

- **Passive bench.** `bench [SECS]` measures whatever is already
  streaming — no device writes, no restore — default 5 s. This
  removes the configure→measure→restore dance that is bench's
  current bulk and its only wedge surface.
- **Decomposed config verbs**, each owning one register concern:
    - `get-hz` / `set-hz=<HZ>` — reg 7 async rate (exists today).
    - `get-ascii` / `set-ascii=<MODE|off>` — reg 6 ASCII preset.
    - `get-bin` / `set-bin=<FIELDS|off>` — reg 75 binary output.
    - Bare `set-bin` enables binary with the *current* fields;
      bare `set-ascii` enables ASCII with the current preset.
- **One connection per invocation** — the whole line opens the
  port once and runs its steps in order. See
  [Step grammar](#step-grammar-joined-tokens-space-separated-steps).
- **Universal rate.** `set-hz=N` is a desired rate independent of
  output mode. See
  [Universal rate (option A)](#universal-rate-option-a).
- **File-backed named states.** A TOML config holds named
  profiles. See [Named states](#named-states-file-backed--default).

### Step grammar: +-joined tokens, space-separated steps

The CLI line is one open connection and a sequence of steps. The
grammar rides on shell word-splitting so there is no custom
space handling.

- Each shell *word* is one **step**; steps execute left-to-right.
- Within a word, tokens joined by `+` apply together as one
  atomic unit; multiple `set-*` in the same word merge into a
  single device write.
- Comma stays reserved for value-internal lists
  (`set-bin=time,accel`); `+` is the only token joiner — never
  comma — so the grammar stays unambiguous and flat (the
  comma-nested alternative would make a value contain
  sub-assignments, i.e. recursion).
- Intra-step order is also left-to-right: `set-hz=50+bench` =
  apply, then measure.
- This unlocks a single-connection **sweep**:
  `save-state set-hz=50+bench set-hz=100+bench set-hz=200+bench
  restore-state` — snapshot, three configure-and-measure steps,
  restore, all on one open.

### Universal rate (option A)

`set-hz=N` reads as one desired rate regardless of mode; the
tool maps it to the right register.

- ASCII: writes reg 7 directly.
- Binary: the rate is the rateDivisor *inside* reg 75 (`800/N`),
  the same register as the field mask — so `set-bin=…+set-hz=N`
  must resolve to one combined reg-75 write. The `+`-join is what
  makes that explicit: same word → one write.
- Bare `set-bin` with no `set-hz` in the same step keeps the
  device's current divisor.

### Named states (file-backed) + default

The `--config` file (default `./.rdwr_vn100-config.toml`, TOML,
hand-editable) holds a map of named profiles plus a `default`
key. Three verbs, three distinct jobs:

- `save-state[=NAME]` — capture the current *device* regs into
  NAME (or the default name).
- `set-state=NAME+set-…` — define NAME from *explicit* values,
  no device read.
- `restore-state[=NAME]` — apply NAME (or the default when no
  name) *to* the device.
- `default` means only "the profile bare `restore-state` uses" —
  **never** auto-applied. The tool stays passive by default;
  nothing writes to the device unless a `set-*`/`restore-state`
  verb asks. We think an auto-applied default would reintroduce
  the surprise-mutation path Issue #1 [[1]] warns about.
- The snapshot covers output config (reg 6 / 7 / 75). Baud
  (reg 5) is excluded from restore by default, because rewriting
  baud is itself the wedge trigger; `--baud` controls the link
  instead.

### Open questions (resolve before coding the relevant step)

- `set-bin` and `set-ascii` both present on one line: error
  ("pick one output to bench") vs measure both — leaning error.
- Persist: apply volatile by default (reverts on power cycle,
  matches today's bench), with `--persist` available.
- `reset` / `factory-reset` reboot the device mid-line — constrain
  them to standalone / last.
- Passive binary frame-rate needs a frame length: reuse
  `get-bin`'s reg-75 parser to self-configure, or sniff the
  binary sync byte `0xFA`. v1 may report byte throughput +
  ASCII line rate and add decoded binary rate via the reg-75 read.

[1]: /notes/bugs.md#issue-1--high-baud-reconnect-can-wedge-the-vn-100-uart

## docs: RS-232 wedge root cause + RPi5 TTL port plan

Commits:

Benching the VN-100 baud climb wedged the device at 921600 —
silent at every baud until a power cycle. The investigation traced
the cause to the bench rig's RS-232 link, not the VN-100, and the
real flight target (RPi5) drives the IMU over a clean 3.3 V TTL
link — which reframes both the bug and the next step.

- Bug recorded as [[1]] (bugs.md Issue #1) with the full baud
  matrix and the recovery (power-cycle the VN-100 only; the FTDI
  adapter stays enumerated, which is what proved the wedge is in
  the device, not the host or adapter).
- The VN-100 firmware supports 921600 (Reg 5) and the in-session
  baud switch read cleanly at 921600 — so the device UART can do
  it. Only the cross-process *reopen* over RS-232 wedges. Cause:
  see [RS-232 link analysis](#rs-232-link-analysis).
- Next step: port `rdwr_vn100` to the RPi5 and re-run the baud
  climb on the TTL UART to test whether the wedge is RS-232-only.
  The tool is already portable (pure Rust + `serialport` crate);
  only the default `--port` (`/dev/ttyUSB0`, `main.rs:463`) is
  bench-specific. On the Pi the line is `/dev/ttyAMA0` (or the
  `/dev/serial0` symlink) — reachable today via `--port`. RPi5
  UART setup: free the line from the serial console
  (`enable_uart=1`, disable the console getty); 3.3 V logic only.
- On the Pi the IMU read path lives in existing Python (primary
  app `../fc/src/fc.py`, plus `../fc/scripts`). We do *not* need
  to port the whole app now, and we do *not* need the VectorNav
  C++ SDK — `rdwr_vn100` already replaces it by talking the
  VN-100 binary protocol directly in Rust. Near-term scope is
  just that: read the VN-100 from Rust over the Pi's TTL link.
  How it then feeds `fc.py` (IPC or a PyO3 module) is a later
  boundary question; a full Rust rewrite (the `fcbr` direction)
  is wanted eventually, not now.

### RS-232 link analysis

The bench adapter is a Gearmo GM-FTD12-LED-C — a USB-C to true
RS-232 adapter (±5.7 V output, rated 300 bps–460 Kbps on the spec
sheet; the "1 Mbps" is marketing-page only). The data lines are
RS-232 levels, so there is no TTL logic-level mismatch.

- Wiring: the VN-100 is on its RS-232 port (data crossed,
  adapter 2/3 ↔ VN-100 3/2), powered from a separate 5 V bench
  supply, common ground, no handshake lines.
- 921600 is ~2× the adapter's 460 Kbps rating. We think RS-232
  slew-rate limiting — on the adapter's transceiver *and* the
  VN-100's own onboard RS-232 transceiver, neither swappable —
  rounds the ±V waveform enough at 921600 (1.08 µs/bit) on the
  hand-made, unterminated cable to mis-frame bytes into the
  VN-100. Its firmware reacts to malformed framing by wedging.
- 57600 / 115200 / 230400 sit within the rating and reconnect
  cleanly; 921600 is 2/2 wedged. 230400 wedged once in a chained
  run with no power cycle between switches, so it is borderline.
- The RPi5 flight target avoids all of this: a direct 3.3 V TTL
  UART has no RS-232 transceivers in the path. We think the wedge
  will not reproduce on TTL; the port-to-Pi step is how we find
  out. Independent of that, binary output at 115200 already
  delivers 200 Hz, so high baud is not required for the flight
  goal — it is headroom, not a blocker.


# References

[1]: /notes/bugs.md#issue-1--high-baud-reconnect-can-wedge-the-vn-100-uart

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).


## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

   grammar run over a single connection, and file-backed named

       ASCII line-count + byte throughput, binary rate via a

content.text:
Plan's sound, and the sync path exists — at session start my repo showed `main@origin`, so the two clones share an `origin` remote. Here's how I read it and a couple of flags.

## Sequence
1. **You (rpi5):** commit the version/edition bump, push `main` to origin.
2. **Me (3900x), after you confirm pushed:** `jj git fetch` to pull the new `main`, then `jj rebase -d main` to move my notes `@` on top of it, then edit the ladder `0.2.0-N` → `0.3.0-N`.

I'll **wait for your "pushed"** before touching anything — no point fetching a half-synced state.

## Why it should go smoothly
- **Rebase is conflict-free.** Your commit touches `Cargo.toml`; my `@` touches only `notes/chores/chores-01.md` and `notes/todo.md`. Disjoint files → clean rebase.
- **Only `todo.md` needs the version edit.** The ladder lives there (`0.2.0-N` → `0.3.0-N`, close-out `0.2.0` → `0.3.0`). The chores section carries **no** version by convention (titles have no `(version)` suffix), so its anchor and the `[[2]]` ref are untouched — no dangling link.
- **My uncommitted `@` becomes the `0.3.0-0` prep commit** naturally — the design note + todo entry *is* the prep content. So the rebase doesn't strand anything.

## Two things to check on your side before you push
1. **Does edition 2024 actually build?** The jump 2021→2024 isn't free — it can need code changes (reserved `gen`, `unsafe` attribute syntax, closure-capture changes) and bumps the MSRV. Run the cargo cycle (fmt → clippy → test → build) on the rpi5 before pushing, so `main` doesn't land red.
2. **Confirm the version you're committing.** You said "0.2.1" earlier but `jj diff` reads ambiguously (the color-words `0.2.01` could be `0.2.0` *or* `0.2.1`). Doesn't change my side — I'm going to `0.3.0` either way — but pin it so the version-of-record is unambiguous for whoever reads it next.

One open question for later (not now): the two clones have diverged into a notes-vs-code split, and you'll likely keep doing flight work on the rpi5. Worth deciding which clone is "primary" for ongoing work so we don't keep re-reconciling — but that's after this rebase settles.

Ready when you've pushed.
